ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for luncheon meat bun; mapped to broader bread product class. Luncheon meat bun is a filled bread roll with processed pork luncheon meat.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for luncheon meat bun; mapped to A000E (Bread and similar products) as the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for luncheon meat bun as a composite food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard luncheon meat bun weighs approximately 120 g and is commonly consumed as a single serving for breakfast or a light lunch in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Luncheon Meat Bun — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Luncheon Meat Bun (F05020063). All core macros and secondary nutrients are from this primary source at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — white bread GI reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for luncheon meat bun as a composite product. GI estimated at 70 based on white bread / soft white bread roll analogue (GI ~70–75, University of Sydney GI Database). The bun component dominates the carbohydrate content (33.8 g/100g); luncheon meat contributes negligible carbohydrate. GL per serving calculated as: GI (70) × carbohydrate per serving (40.56 g for 120 g serving) / 100 = 28.4, rounded to 28.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 439 mg/100g from processed luncheon meat and bread, with insufficient potassium or fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid luncheon meat buns due to 439 mg sodium per 100 g (527 mg per standard 120 g serving), which represents over 23% of the WHO daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; the processed luncheon meat filling contributes sodium nitrates and preservatives that further elevate cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic index (~70) refined white flour bun with 33.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g and low dietary fibre at 2.1 g/100g, promoting rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit luncheon meat buns because the refined white flour base has a high GI (~70) and delivers approximately 40.6 g of rapidly absorbed carbohydrate per standard serving, causing sharp postprandial glucose spikes; the saturated fat from processed luncheon meat also worsens insulin resistance over time.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Ultra-processed food with high sodium (439 mg/100g), saturated fat (~4.5 g/100g), and sodium nitrates from processed luncheon meat linked to increased CVD risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid luncheon meat buns as the combination of high sodium, saturated fat from processed pork luncheon meat, and refined carbohydrates creates a compounded cardiovascular risk profile consistent with AHA dietary guidelines recommending limitation of ultra-processed and high-sodium foods.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory processed pork luncheon meat with sodium nitrites and saturated fat, combined with high-glycaemic refined flour bun
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid luncheon meat buns as the processed pork filling contains sodium nitrites and arachidonic acid (omega-6) that amplify inflammatory pathways, while the refined white flour bun drives glycaemic-mediated inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Luncheon meat bun is a popular Singaporean convenience food consisting of a soft, slightly sweet white bread bun filled with sliced processed pork luncheon meat (commonly a Spam-type product). The bun is typically baked from enriched white wheat flour with sugar and yeast, yielding a pillowy, mildly sweet crumb. The luncheon meat filling is salty, savoury, and dense, providing a contrasting flavour and texture to the soft bread. This item is widely sold at kopitiams (traditional Singaporean coffee shops), bakeries, convenience stores, and school canteens, and is commonly consumed as a breakfast item or a working-lunch snack. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides 299 kcal, 10.6 g protein, 13.51 g fat, and 33.8 g carbohydrate with only 2.1 g dietary fibre. Sodium content is high at 439 mg/100g, primarily from the processed luncheon meat. The food is classified as ultra-processed due to the luncheon meat component, which contains preservatives, sodium nitrates, and added salt. Clinically, this food is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease due to its high sodium, high glycaemic index (~70), and saturated fat content. It is not halal-certified as standard luncheon meat contains pork.
common_user_queries: What is a luncheon meat bun?
How many calories in a luncheon meat bun?
Can diabetics eat luncheon meat bun?
Is luncheon meat bun high in sodium?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F05020063) at confidence 0.95. The food is a canonical Singaporean convenience food with an exact match in the primary regional source. GI is estimated from a well-established white bread analogue from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the only approved GI source; this introduces a minor estimation caveat but does not reduce overall confidence to medium given the dominance of the white flour bun in the carbohydrate profile.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g set to 4.5 g/100g: estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite data for luncheon meat bun (white bread + canned luncheon meat); HPB source does not separately report saturated fat for this item. This is an adapted estimate and should be replaced with a direct HPB measurement if available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this composite food item as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on white bread / soft white bread roll from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for luncheon meat bun was found in approved sources. 4. Halal status is false by default as standard commercial luncheon meat contains pork; halal-certified variants exist but are not the default product. 5. is_kosher set to false as pork-containing products are not kosher.
